title: Hollowing a spoon bowl with a gouge
subtitle: Using a No. 7 sweep gouge to rough out a spoon bowl before the hook knife
excerpt: A gouge roughing pass before the hook knife saves time on large spoon bowls and kuksa interiors. This technique covers grip, sweep selection, mallet use, and the transition from gouge to hook knife finish. It is the faster method for any bowl deeper than 1.5 cm.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for gouge-and-mallet hollowing method; Newey and Drage Practical Carpentry for sweep selection and grain direction rules.

glossaryTerms.definition: The curvature of a gouge cutting edge, numbered from 1 (flat) to 11 (deep U). A No. 7 sweep is the standard bowl-roughing gouge.
Blade travelling away from the body into the wood, driven by hand or mallet pressure.
A curved-bladed knife for fine-finishing a hollowed bowl after roughing.

content.text: A
hook knife

is the standard tool for finishing a spoon bowl, but for bowls deeper than 1.5 cm; ladles, kuksa, large serving spoons; a gouge roughing pass removes material faster and with less effort. The gouge works mallet-driven rather than hand-pushed, which means it can take heavier cuts without the hand fatigue that builds over a long hook-knife session.
When to use it

Use the gouge roughing pass on any bowl deeper than 1.5 cm or wider than 7 cm. For a standard eating spoon with a 5 cm oval bowl and 1 cm depth, the hook knife is faster. For a ladle, a kuksa, or a large salad serving bowl, the gouge rough-out first saves 20 to 30 minutes of hook-knife work.
What you need
A No. 7 sweep gouge in 12 mm width is the standard bowl-roughing gouge. A 20 mm version removes material faster on large pieces but is harder to control. The
gouge sweep

must be sharp; a dull gouge driven by a mallet will split rather than cut green wood. A carpenter's mallet is the preferred driver; a heavy wooden club beetle works but gives less control. Secure the blank to a workbench or chopping block before starting. Never hold the blank in one hand while driving the gouge with the other.

The wood state matters. Green wood is much easier to remove with a gouge than seasoned; the gouge is the preferred roughing tool specifically for green-wood bowls. On seasoned wood the grain can split ahead of the gouge rather than cutting cleanly.
Step-by-step
Mark the bowl outline on the blank face in pencil. Mark the target depth; the bowl floor; on the side face.
Clamp or pin the blank to the bench with the bowl face up. The blank must not move under mallet blows.

Start the gouge at the rim of the bowl, bevel-down, cutting straight across the grain toward the bowl centre. A single mallet stroke drives the gouge 5 to 8 mm into the wood. Work around the rim in short overlapping passes, always cutting toward the centre of the bowl. Cutting from the centre outward goes against the grain and will lift the floor rather than cut it.

Work in rings from the rim inward, removing one ring of material per pass. The bowl interior after the gouge should be a stepped cone; uniform steps, not random gouges. Remove 3 to 4 mm of depth per ring.
Stop when you are 3 mm from the target depth. The final 3 mm is finish work for the
hook knife
; the gouge cannot reach the bowl floor cleanly without risk of over-cutting.
Switch to the hook knife. The hook knife removes the gouge steps and brings the bowl to a smooth, consistent surface. Use a
push cut
arc; rim toward centre; in short strokes of 2 to 3 cm.
What it looks like when it is right

A correctly executed gouge rough-out leaves a bowl interior that looks like a shallow stepped funnel: even steps, consistent depth around each ring, no tearing or splitting of the grain. The steps are clean-cut, not ragged. When the hook knife transitions smoothly between the steps without catching, the rough-out was done correctly.
Common mistakes
Driving the gouge from the bowl centre outward: this goes against the grain and tears the wood ahead of the cut. Always cut from the rim toward the centre.
Going past the target depth in one pass: a mallet-driven gouge can easily overshoot. Take smaller passes; 3 mm depth; and check frequently.
Skipping the hook knife finish: the gouge leaves a faceted surface that the hook knife must smooth. A bowl finished with only the gouge will retain step marks.
Using a dull gouge: a dull gouge driven by a mallet splits the wood rather than cutting it. Check the edge shaves hair from the forearm before starting.
